title: Simple Rustic Modern Console Table
summary: A minimal four-board console with an inset 1x10 back panel between the legs — just a top, two legs, a shelf, and a recessed back board, no additional bracing anywhere.
description:
This console pares a console table down to its barest structural minimum: a top, two solid 1x12 legs, a bottom shelf, and a single inset 1x10 back board — four boards, no aprons, no separate stretchers. Attach the top to the legs first with either brad nails and glue or pocket holes, a choice the plan leaves genuinely open since either works fine at this scale.

The back board is the one piece worth some care: it sets in flush between the two legs rather than spanning across their outside faces, which is what gives the piece its clean, inset look from the front. **This inset back board is doing real structural work despite its plain appearance** — it's the only piece tying the two legs together besides the top and shelf, so getting it snug against both legs matters more than it looks like it should on a four-board table.

The bottom shelf attaches to the sides and the back board last, closing up the whole structure. Because there's no separate apron or stretcher system, keeping every joint square as you go is what keeps the finished piece from racking — there's no second chance to add bracing after the fact without it showing.

materials.name: Brad nails, 1-1/4", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

steps.title: Cut the four boards
steps.body: From the 1x12, cut the top 48", the shelf 46 1/2" and two legs 29 1/4", all at the board's full 11 1/4" width. Check the board before you start: those four pieces come to 153" and a nominal 12 ft 1x12 is 144", so one board will not yield them — plan on a second board or longer stock. From the 4 ft 1x10, cut the back panel 46 1/2" long at its full 9 1/4" width, which leaves about 1 1/2" spare. Square every cut, and check the shelf and the back panel against each other before assembly: both are 46 1/2" and both have to sit between the same two legs, so any difference between them shows up as a gap you cannot close.

steps.title: Attach the top to the legs
steps.body: Stand the two 29 1/4" legs on edge, 46 1/2" apart on the inside, and cap them with the 48" top so it finishes flush with the outside face of each leg — 46 1/2" plus 3/4" of leg at each end is exactly 48". Fasten with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue, or with pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws drilled into the leg tops so nothing shows on the surface. This table has no apron and no stretcher, so square matters more than usual: check both diagonals and both leg-to-top corners before the glue sets. Finished, the console stands 30" tall — 29 1/4" of leg under a 3/4" top.
materials: Brad nails, 1-1/4", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: Set the 9 1/4" x 46 1/2" 1x10 between the two legs rather than across their outside faces. Sitting flush between them is what gives the piece its clean inset look from the front, and it is also the only part besides the top and shelf tying the two legs together, so it is doing real structural work. Push it tight against both legs before fastening — a back board even a hair short leaves the console free to rack. Glue both ends and nail through the legs into the panel's ends, or pocket-screw it from behind where the holes will not show.
materials: Brad nails, 1-1/4", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

steps.title: Attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Slide the 46 1/2" shelf in between the legs and land it on the bottom edge of the back panel so panel and shelf support each other. Mark the shelf height on both legs from the same reference end so it goes in level, then fasten through the legs into the shelf's ends and along the back edge into the panel, with glue at every joint. Check both diagonals one last time: this is the joint that locks the four-board structure, and there is no way to add bracing afterwards without it showing.
materials: Brad nails, 1-1/4", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Fill any nail holes, sand every face and ease the top's edges, then apply primer and paint, or stain and poly. Finish the underside of the top and the back of the panel too — a board sealed on only one face can cup as the seasons change, and on a four-board console there is nothing holding a cupped board flat.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Minimal four-board console table with an inset back panel
